# Results ledger (auto-generated — do not edit by hand)

Generated by `scripts/make_results_ledger.py` from the committed `results/*.csv`; regenerate after the pipeline. Every value here is read from an output file, so it is guaranteed consistent with the code. All absolute numbers are PRELIMINARY where noted — they ride on the OPEN beam waist $w_0$ (README §2.5). Frequencies are transition-axis unless a `_LASER` note says otherwise.

> **The dominant systematic is the OPEN beam waist $w_0$ — only the fixed-lock session knife-edge measures it.** The M9 transit Monte-Carlo flux bug (it ran ~2× too narrow) was FIXED and fully propagated on 2026-07-13: the corrected transit excludes the old 32 µm nominal (natural convolved with transit alone already exceeds the observed ~5.25 MHz line there) and re-centres $w_0$ to **~50 µm** (45–70; hard floor 38). Every $w_0$-conditional number below (the $\beta_\text{self}$ and $\sigma_\text{laser}$ bounds, $S_0$, the $w_0$-band) has been RE-RUN at the corrected prior; they stay PRELIMINARY and $w_0$-conditional because the transit vs $\sigma_\text{laser}$ degeneracy means the archival line cannot pin $w_0$ itself — that is the knife-edge's job. An earlier note inferred $w_0\approx90$ µm from a second (spurious) factor-of-2; retracted — see `docs/notes/transit_width_resolved.md`, `literature_food_for_thought.md`.

## C1 — collisional self-broadening $\beta_\text{self}$

Three estimators of the SAME quantity under DIFFERENT $\sigma_\text{laser}$
treatments. They span a **factor $\sim$2** (0.03–0.06) — that
model-dependence IS the C1 uncertainty, larger than any single fit's error
bar, and it is the honest headline. The **conservative model-independent
bound ($<\sim$0.15) is the robust floor**; the fits are PRELIMINARY
cross-checks pending a fixed-lock session.

| estimator | value (MHz per $10^{12}$ cm$^{-3}$) | status |
|---|---|---|
| model-independent width-slope (95% bound, per peak: $t(1)=6.31\times$syst, then $\times1.2$ density-scale) | 993.4121 nm $<$ 0.21; 993.4154 nm $<$ 0.44; 993.4192 nm $<$ 0.25; 993.4207 nm $<$ 0.42 | **headline BOUND** (geometry-robust) |

> Bound construction (corrected 2026-07-16 — two coverage fixes). (i) **Student-t, not 2σ:** the between-block scatter that dominates the slope error is estimated on only **1 residual DOF** (3 density points, 2 fit params), so a one-sided 95% needs $t(0.95, 1) = 6.31$, not the Gaussian-asymptotic 2 used before — the old “$\approx$2σ” bounds (0.07–0.15) under-covered, which is why they were always flagged with a ~factor-2 own-uncertainty; the t-quantile formalizes exactly that. (ii) **Density scale:** $\beta \propto 1/N$, so the ~20% spread between published vapor-pressure correlations moves every $\beta$ by 20%; the cold-spot direction makes the fitted $\beta$ an UNDERestimate, so the bound is inflated on the + side ($\times$1.2; `density.py`). **The per-peak spread is systematics, not physics:** the bounds track each peak's `resid_rms` (0.13/0.35/0.18/0.29), not a physical rate — all four have SNR $<$ 2 (none resolves collisions). Because the min of noisy 1-DOF estimates is the down-fluctuated one, the **loosest** peak is the honest single-number floor, not the tightest (that would be selection bias); quote the range, floor at the loosest. The cross-session 130 °C lever variant (dof = 2, in the CSV) is far tighter but carries its documented cross-session caveat.

| per-peak fits ($\sigma_\text{laser}$ free per peak) | 993.4121 nm 0.019(8); 993.4154 nm 0.016(8); 993.4192 nm 0.019(4); 993.4207 nm 0.034(6) | PRELIM (993.4207 nm ~1.6σ above 993.4121 nm) |
| hierarchical ($\sigma_\text{laser}$ shared per-T across peaks; $\beta$ per isotope) | 85Rb 0.035(4); 87Rb 0.036(4) | PRELIM — the (·) is **STAT-only**; the dominant systematic (the unverifiable per-T timing assumption, below) is unquantified, so this is not a %-precise measurement |

> Why the hierarchical $\beta$ can exceed BOTH per-peak values (not a contradiction): it is not a pooling of them. Sharing one $\sigma_\text{laser}(T)$ across the four peaks pins the laser width lower at high density (110 °C), so more width is assigned to collisions than when each peak floats its own $\sigma_\text{laser}$. But that per-T sharing **assumes the four peaks at a given T saw the same laser width** — i.e. were acquired close in time — which the archive cannot verify (timestamps were not logged; round-5 caveat). So the hierarchical 0.036 is conditional on an **unverifiable acquisition-timing assumption**, which is the real reason it is a cross-check and the conservative model-independent bound is the headline — not merely that it is 'optimistic'. All fit errors are marginal (full covariance), folding in the $\beta \leftrightarrow \sigma_\text{laser}$ anticorrelation; the estimator spread is the ADDITIONAL model systematic.

> **The lever cross-check (M4d, `run_lever_crosscheck`) — a lever-limited cross-check ESTIMATOR, not the headline $\beta$.** *(Renamed from the misleading `definitive_fit`: it is a cross-check — do not grab `lever_crosscheck.csv` as the headline; that is `beta_self_probe.csv`.)* The model-independent width-slope bound (top row) is the headline; this row is the internally-consistent **70/90/110 °C cooling-sweep** joint fit (one session), whose central value the lever test below shows to be a *bound*, not a measurement. It uses the Lehmann-cusp transit — the **established** Biraben–Cagnac kernel (App. B), so a physics choice, not a coin flip; the Gaussian/Voigt alternative is the deliberate cusp-free *null*, and its $\beta$ spread is the model-form bar. Sharing is per-$T$ $\sigma_\text{laser}$ (M4c-*consistent* — an **in-sample** check, §C2). Its statistical precision and audited systematics per isotope:

| isotope | $\beta$ | statistical | model-form (transit / sharing) | $w_0$-band | drop-a-peak |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 85Rb | 0.0355 | $\pm$0.0036 | $\pm$0.033 (0.033 / 0.001) | [0.003, 0.055] | 0.0069 (peak 4192) |
| 87Rb | 0.0360 | $\pm$0.0041 | $\pm$0.034 (0.032 / 0.002) | [0.004, 0.055] | 0.0049 (peak 4207) |
> **Read the $\pm0.004$ as this estimator's precision, not $\beta$'s.** It is the cooling-sweep joint-fit covariance — but $\beta$ rides on only **three density points**, so the slope lever is fragile (few effective DOF, heavy-tailed — the same $\sim$factor-2 own-uncertainty the model-independent bound carries), and the **lever test below moves this central value by $\sim8\sigma$**. So 0.036 is a bound wearing an estimator's error budget: treat it as the short-lever end of a lever-dependent range (per-condition $\sim$0.01 → cooling 0.036), and the $\pm0.004$/model-form/$w_0$ bars as the precision and systematics *of this estimator*, which do not span the bound. The headline number stays the top-row width-slope bound.

> Per-drop detail is committed (`beta_loo_drop`/`sigma_loo_drop` rows): dropping the 993.4207 nm suspect moves $\beta_{87}$ by -0.0049 (below its statistical error) and leaves the $\sigma_\text{laser}(T)$ pattern intact (1.46/1.71/1.03 vs 1.48/1.63/1.06 with all peaks) — the suspect drives **neither** the coefficient **nor** the $\sigma_\text{laser}$ trend, whose 110 °C dip M4c already attributes to the $\beta\leftrightarrow\sigma_\text{laser}$ degeneracy. The same holds for every other single-peak drop.


> The **$w_0$-band is the largest systematic** (the OPEN waist — the knife-edge measurement collapses it), then the transit model-form (Lehmann cusp vs Voigt); the $\sigma_\text{laser}$ A-vs-B *sharing* choice is minor — per-block (Model B) gives $\beta_{87}=0.034$ vs per-$T$ 0.036, agreeing to $\sim$0.002, so the per-$T$ headline (the M4c-consistent, physically-motivated choice) is *robust to it*, not a coin flip. **Drop-a-peak is small** — no single peak, including the 993.4207 nm suspect, drives $\beta$. (Drop-a-*temperature* is large — up to 0.09 if present — but that is expected **lever leverage**: removing one of only three temperatures shortens the density arm; it is a sensitivity diagnostic, not a robustness failure.) A synthetic-injection closure (`tests/test_lever_crosscheck`) confirms the 20-trace machinery recovers a known $\beta$.

> **Lever test — $\beta$ is a bound, not a value, because $\gamma_\text{coll}$ barely grows with density.** Adding the curated 130 °C anchor (`serves_t130`, 225 mW; density lever $\times16\to\times53$) pulls the joint $\beta$ to [85Rb 0.014; 87Rb 0.016] — well below the cooling-sweep 0.036 and larger than every error bar above. This is **not** one bad block: the per-condition $\gamma_\text{coll}$ rises only $\times$1.9 across the $\times$52 density span (70→130 °C), and the 130 °C widths sit *on* that near-flat trend — whereas a real binary-collision width is **linear** in $N$. So the fitted $\gamma_\text{coll}$ is a residual **floor**, not resolved collisions, and the apparent $\beta$ shrinks as the lever lengthens (per-condition $\beta$ is only $\sim$0.01; the joint 0.036 is $\sigma_\text{laser}$-sharing-inflated). That is precisely why $\beta$ is a BOUND. The 130 °C data are also a different session — a secondary, unseparable caveat — so a fixed-lock session needs *same-session* high-density points to resolve any real collisional slope.

*Lifted by:* a fixed lock (removes between-block laser drift) + knife-edge $w_0$ (sets the transit subtraction) → a clean measurement.

## C2 — 2025 laser-epoch width $\sigma_\text{laser}$

- **<1.0 MHz (laser axis)** — UPPER_BOUND@w0prior; =0 at w0~16um; unmeasurable pending knife-edge.
- Degenerate with $w_0$ via transit (M9): the corrected transit adds ~2.1 MHz at $w_0=32$ µm (which OVERSHOOTS the observed line, so 32 µm is excluded) and ~1.2 MHz at the 50 µm prior; more transit leaves less width for the laser.

> **Reconciling the three $\sigma_\text{laser}$ numbers (they are not contradictory — mind the axis).** This C2 bound is $<$1.0 MHz on the **laser** axis; the lever-crosscheck $\sigma_\text{laser}(T)$ (1.48/1.63/1.06) and the Stark-fit core (0.52–0.82) are on the **transition** axis ($=2\times$ laser). Halved, the lever gives laser $\approx$0.53–0.82 and the Stark core $\approx$0.26–0.41 MHz, both under this bound. The 70/90 °C tied values (1.48/1.63 transition) are inflated by the $\beta\leftrightarrow\sigma_\text{laser}$ degeneracy (the free per-condition fit is flatter, $\sim$1.0–1.25 transition); the underlying laser width is $\sim$1.1 transition $=$ ~0.55 laser. Every committed CSV carries the axis in its unit string.

*Lifted by:* knife-edge $w_0$ + a direct linewidth measurement in a fixed-lock session.

## C3 — power sweep (ramp-law predictions confirmed)

- **C3a linewidth flat:** inflation 3–8% across 25–225 mW, non-monotonic (block scatter, not power broadening).
- **C3b amplitude $\propto P^2$:** log-log slopes 1.83–2.12 (two-photon rate law; 993.4121 nm mildly saturates). The power slope is immune to radiation trapping (even at the thick $\tau/$cm$\approx$160): the trapping optical depth is set by the GROUND-state density, which the weak two-photon excitation does not deplete, so the trapping factor is power-independent at fixed T and rescales the amplitude without bending the log-log slope. (This is the operative condition — ground-state non-depletion — not a linear-reabsorption assumption.) This immunity is an ARGUMENT, not tested here: the archival sweep is single-temperature (130 °C, one $\tau$), so slope-vs-$\tau$ cannot be checked — multi-T power sweeps in a fixed-lock session would. 993.4121 nm's low slope (1.83) is unresolved: dim-line SNR bias at 25 mW, genuine saturation, or a weak power-dependence of trapping at the thick end are not separable from one single-T sweep.
- **C3c — the RAMP asymmetry is a bound; the significant low-power skew in `power_sweep.csv` is shot noise, not the ramp.** Two DISTINCT quantities, and conflating them is a trap. (i) The **ramp** skew (the asymmetry coefficient of the fitted ramp$\otimes$core profile, $\propto S_0^3$) is below the SNR floor at $\le$225 mW → an **upper bound**, consistent with zero, as the corrected physics requires (the ramp's first-order shift is absorbed by the per-scan free centre; §THEORY_NOTE 3). (ii) The committed `resid_skew` column is a *different* thing — the residual skewness of a **symmetric**-model fit — and it is large and POSITIVE at low power (up to **~10σ** at 25 mW: 993.4154 nm 0.345±0.036) and **falls** with amplitude as $\sim$amp$^{-0.5}$. That is the Poisson **shot-noise skewness** (the noise is right-skewed $\propto 1/\sqrt{\text{counts}}$, positive, vanishing as the line brightens) — a statistical artifact of the noise, NOT a physical line asymmetry, and the *opposite* power dependence of the ramp ($\propto P^3$ growth). So the 9σ is real but identified: `resid_skew` is a shot-noise-dominated diagnostic, not a ramp signal, and it is why the archival skew cannot serve as a ramp measurement. (Per-peak scaling exponent $-0.2$ to $-0.9$ about the $-0.5$ shot-noise value, so a small extra low-power systematic — baseline, line-pull — is not excluded, but shot noise dominates.) a fixed-lock session lifts the real observable two ways: the fixed lock un-absorbs the large first-order pull, and the small waist ($S_0$ 4× larger, skew up to 64×) makes the ramp asymmetry a detection — conditional on the §7 geometry correction. See docs/THEORY_NOTE.md §3.

- **C3d — the AC-Stark coefficient, from the power-lever: an UPPER BOUND $S_0$(225 mW) $< 0.63$ MHz (95%, profile likelihood), sitting just above the predicted 0.59 MHz** (`run_stark_sweep`, M4e — the power-lever twin of the $\beta$ fit). One shared $\kappa$ ($S_0=\kappa P$) is fit to the four peaks' FWHM-vs-power (each floating its core width); the ramp broadens the line as $\sim S_0^2$, the only live handle since the *shift* (pull $\sim S_0$) is dead in the 2025 drift. The fit ($S_0(225) = 0.00$, $\kappa$ consistent with zero) is **not** a measurement; it **brackets** the prediction. **The construction of the 95% is load-bearing and documented:** the best fit rails at $\kappa=0$, where the width handle ($\propto S_0^2$) has zero gradient, so a linearized (Wald) $\kappa+1.645\sigma$ bound has no valid coverage there — its $\sigma$ is a finite-difference artifact (the superseded Wald numbers, 1.5 raw / 3.1 inflated MHz, stay in the CSV as diagnostics). The quoted bound is instead a **profile-likelihood** limit: $\kappa$ is scanned upward with the per-peak cores re-minimized at each point, and the limit sits where $\chi^2$ rises by $2.706\times\chi^2_\text{red}$ — the threshold scaling that carries the over-dispersion ($\chi^2_\text{red}\approx4$, block-to-block width scatter) into the bound, conservatively. The result says the archive's width data are already sensitive at the scale of the predicted coefficient: anything much above the prediction is excluded, while the prediction itself (and zero) remain allowed. *Lifted by:* the fixed lock measures the pull $\sim S_0$ directly, and the small waist makes $S_0$ several-fold larger — turning this bound into the coefficient.

## Supporting results

- **Frequency axis (M2):** 0.04257(5) MHz/ms laser-axis (~0.12%). The $\chi^2_\text{red}=6.8$ over 20 blocks is genuine over-dispersion, and the quoted error is **already** PDG-inflated by $\sqrt{\chi^2_\text{red}}\approx2.6\times$ to absorb it (verified: $5.0\times10^{-5} = 1.9\times10^{-5}\times2.6$). The over-dispersion is **block-level ruler scatter** — drift between the before/after brackets, plus a likely outlier (the 993.4207 nm *after* block at 0.0432, ~1.4% above its own *before*) — **not** a peak-ordered axis distortion: bracket-resolved, the per-peak rates are non-monotonic and the two brackets disagree on the ordering (a naive pool of before+after fakes a monotonic trend). So it inflates the COMMON axis error symmetrically and does NOT bias the cross-peak differential comparisons (β₈₅ vs β₈₇, the M10 ratios). The fits use each condition's own block rate; sweep linear <0.4% within a block. **Leave-it-out (drop-a-block rigor):** dropping the flagged 993.4207 nm *after* block shifts the campaign rate by only 0.08% (0.70σ, 0.04257→0.04254) and $\chi^2_\text{red}$ 6.8→3.8 — it is the dominant scatter contributor but the PDG inflation already absorbs it, so the rate is robust to it. Kept per policy (statistical outliers are review-only, not auto-cut), now with the leave-out on record.
- **σ_laser sharing (M4c) — an IN-SAMPLE consistency check, not a proof:** at 70/90/110 °C the four peak-blocks agree on one σ_laser (χ²/dof 0.2/0.6/0.3, all $<$1), consistent with the per-T sharing the global fit assumes. Two honest limits: (i) agreement is **necessary, not sufficient** — the four peaks could have co-drifted between acquisitions and still agree; only logged timestamps (a fixed-lock session) settle it. (ii) It is **in-sample**: the check uses 70/90/110 °C and the headline fit uses the *same* three temperatures, so it cannot be an out-of-sample test — the 130 °C block that would stress the assumption is a different session (excluded, §C1 lever test). (iii) **The test is under-powered:** χ²/dof of 0.21/0.64/0.33 are *well below 1*, so the peaks agree only within error bars that are themselves $\sim$1.2–2.2× too large (the conservative $\tau_\text{int}$ noise inflation over-estimates them). At these χ² the data cannot discriminate shared from unshared — so the sharing is **untested**, not merely unverifiable. So it answers the round-5 timing worry as *consistency*, not confirmation. But the *free* per-condition σ_laser is $\sim$flat (1.6–1.8), while the β·N-tied global fit inflates it to 2.1–2.2 and drops it to 1.60 at 110 °C: that σ_laser(T) trend is the **β↔σ_laser degeneracy** under the density constraint, NOT a physical laser drift — so the '110 °C anomaly' is a model artifact, not a stale block, and it does not corrupt β (the density lever still pins the collisional slope). Reproducible: `run_sigma_laser_sharing.py`.
- **Lehmann cusp (M8):** ΔBIC(Voigt−Lehmann) -0.1…+3.6 — no preference for the cusp (the negative end is a mild dispreference on 993.4207 nm), all far below the gate ≥10; needs the fixed-lock session's narrow laser.
- **Radiation trapping (M7):** amplitude ∝ N, log-log slopes 0.85–1.02 (no significant rollover), though the cell is optically THICK on D1 (τ/cm≈1–160) — trapping redistributes rather than destroys the collected 795 nm signal. Degeneracy-breaking (peak-differential) trapping is sought but sub-detection: ⁸⁵Rb is 0.09±0.08 more sublinear than ⁸⁷Rb (right sign, ~1σ); the 30–50% ratio disagreement (M10) is NON-monotonic in density ⇒ drift, not trapping.
- **Degeneracy law (M10):** areas should be abundance×(2F+1) (5/3, 7/5 within-isotope); measured ratios swing 30–50% between blocks (drift) — not testable in the archive; a fixed-lock session: interleave peaks.
